title: SEC approves first spot Bitcoin ETF for U.S. markets, triggering record institutional inflows
topicContext: BTC/USD is the benchmark cryptocurrency pair. Spot Bitcoin ETF approval has been the single most anticipated regulatory catalyst in crypto history, expected to unlock institutional capital that was previously unable to hold Bitcoin directly. The pair traded between $42,000 and $48,000 in the weeks before the decision. A spot ETF allows pension funds, endowments, and registered investment advisors to gain Bitcoin exposure through a traditional brokerage account without custodying the asset themselves.
body:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on Wednesday approved the first spot Bitcoin exchange-traded fund for listing on American exchanges, ending a decade-long battle between the crypto industry and regulators and opening the door to a wave of institutional money entering the market.

The SEC approved applications from three asset managers simultaneously, with trading expected to begin on Thursday. The approval covers funds that will hold actual Bitcoin rather than futures contracts, a distinction the industry has argued is critical for accurate price tracking and lower costs for investors.

Bitcoin surged 14% to $52,400 within hours of the announcement, its highest level since late 2021. Trading volumes across major exchanges hit record levels, with Coinbase reporting a temporary outage due to demand. The total cryptocurrency market capitalization rose above $2 trillion for the first time in 18 months.

SEC Chair Gary Gensler said in a statement that the approval was 'based on a thorough review of the applications and the current market structure,' but added that 'investors should remain cautious about the risks associated with investing in crypto-related products.' Two commissioners dissented, arguing the market remains too susceptible to manipulation.

Analysts estimate the ETFs could attract between $50 billion and $100 billion in inflows within the first two years. 'This is the most significant moment for Bitcoin since its creation,' said the head of digital assets at a major Wall Street bank. 'You're about to see every financial advisor in America have a conversation about a 1-2% Bitcoin allocation.'

The approval also triggered a sharp move in traditional markets. Gold fell 1.8% as some investors repositioned toward Bitcoin as an alternative store of value. The Nasdaq rose 0.6%, led by crypto-adjacent companies including Coinbase, MicroStrategy, and Bitcoin mining firms. The dollar index was broadly unchanged.

European regulators signaled they are monitoring the development closely but have no immediate plans to follow suit. The European Securities and Markets Authority said it would 'assess the implications for European investors and market stability' before considering any similar approvals.
